title: Study systems
text:
Much of my research is based on long-term demographic monitoring of wildlife populations. These long-term datasets provide a unique opportunity to investigate how endogenous population processes and exogenous stochastic forces interact to shape population dynamics across space and time.
During the last three decades, we have monitored population density and individual demographic traits in gulls, shearwaters and terns across several Mediterranean sites, including the Balearic Islands and the eastern Iberian coastline. By individually marking animals in the field and compiling long-term resighting histories, we build capture-recapture and population models to estimate key demographic processes such as survival, recruitment and dispersal among populations.
The ecosystems I know best are those inhabited by my main study species, particularly Mediterranean coastal habitats, islands and small islets. At the same time, analysing data from a wide diversity of species has broadened my perspective towards other ecosystems, exposed to different environmental conditions, disturbance regimes and conservation challenges. This comparative perspective helps me examine how demographic mechanisms vary across contrasting ecological contexts and life-history strategies.

title: Study systems
text:
Study species

Audouin’s gull
The Audouin’s gull Larus audouinii, is a long-lived, social and bet-hedging species that evolved in ephemeral breeding habitats. Long-term monitoring of Audouin’s gulls in the western Mediterranean started in the late 1980s and now includes tens of thousands of marked individuals and resightings across multiple breeding patches. This system provides exceptional data for studying survival, recruitment, dispersal, breeding-site dynamics, colonisation-extinction processes, responses to rare events and the demographic consequences of anthropogenic change.


Scopoli’s shearwater
Scopoli’s shearwater Calonectris diomedea is a long-lived seabird, with a markedly slow life history strategy. Together with other shearwaters, it provides a contrasting demographic model for exploring how life-history strategies influence vulnerability, resilience and responses to environmental variability and human impacts.


Balearic shearwater and other marine vertebrates
Even if I am not responsable of the species monitoring, I have also worked on other marine species affected by conservation threats such as fisheries bycatch and global change, such as the critically endangered Balearic shearwater Puffinus mauretanicus, and the endangered Leatherback turtle, Dermochelys coriacea.


Other
Beyond seabirds and marine turtles, I have also worked ocassionally with other taxa and systems, including reptiles, mammals, butterflies, fishes and broader comparative demographic datasets. This diversity of systems allows me to examine how demographic mechanisms vary across contrasting life histories, ecological contexts and conservation challenges.

title: People
text: Research is a collective process. This page brings together current and former students and postdoctoral researchers who have worked with me or under my supervision, contributing to different projects. Many other colleagues have also been part of this wider collective effort; they are included on the Collaborations page.

title: Collaborations
text:
My research is strongly based on collaborations with researchers from different fields, including population ecology, evolutionary ecology, conservation biology, ecological modelling, genetics, statistical ecology, physics and mathematics.
I enjoy collaborating with enthusiastic researchers, both within and beyond my own field. Collaborations may grow from shared data, complementary methods, theoretical perspectives or simply from exchanging ideas around a common question. I especially value how this process often leads not only to answers, but also to new and more interesting questions than those we had at the beginning.

title: Research
text:
My research uses quantitative demography to understand how differences among individuals, populations and species shape ecological responses to environmental change across scales. By combining long-term data from natural populations with statistical modelling, simulations and ecological theory, I investigate how life-history strategies, population structure and environmental variability interact to determine vulnerability, resilience and persistence.
A central aim of my work is to move from species-specific demographic responses towards a more general understanding of how life histories and population structure mediate the demographic consequences of environmental change. This perspective helps reveal the mechanisms through which individual variation, population dynamics and species differences scale up to generate emergent patterns of vulnerability, resilience and persistence across levels of biological organisation. At the same time, the detailed knowledge gained from particular species and systems can provide a stronger basis for biodiversity conservation.

title: Research
text:
Research lines


Quantitative demography of structured populations 
Life-history strategies, vulnerability and resilience 
Individual heterogeneity 
Environmental stochasticity, rare events and perturbation regimes 
Spatial demography and metapopulation processes 
Demographic structure and community resilience 
Transient dynamics and nonlinear responses 
Conservation demography 



Keywords:
animal ecology; capture-recapture; community ecology; conservation biology; demography; stability/multistability; global change; individual heterogeneity; life-history evolution; life-history strategies; pace-of-life; population dynamics; population ecology; population structure; population viability analysis; quantitative ecology; resilience; social cohesion; statistical modelling; stochasticity; transient dynamics.

title: Meritxell Genovart
text:
ORCID
Google Scholar
GitHub
CEAB-CSIC
Theelab
Bycatch
Biodiversity Challenges



My research is driven by the aim of understanding how life-history strategies and demographic processes shape ecological and evolutionary responses to environmental change, from individual populations and species to ecological communities. From a quantitative demographic perspective, I am interested in how differences among individuals, populations and species influence vulnerability, resilience and persistence, and how they may scale up to generate emergent patterns in natural systems.
By combining long-term data from natural populations with statistical modelling, simulations and ecological theory, my work seeks to identify the demographic and evolutionary mechanisms underlying ecological change and to contribute to a more predictive ecology for biodiversity conservation.

Demography Population ecology Life-history strategies Conservation biology Biodiversity Resilience


Email: m.genovart@csic.es
Affiliation: Centre d’Estudis Avançats de Blanes (CEAB-CSIC)
